17. Maximum Entropy Distributions

From our development of model capacity, we know the least informative prior is the solution to apotentially high-dimensional convex optimization problem. We noted that the detailed structure of the prior is not critical to achieving good performance, and more importantly it should be strictly positive (lie in the interior of the simplex).

17.1. Maximally Ignorant Priors

Motivation: Suppose we have no data and want to make a “fair” prior.

We choose a prior that is simpler to design and independent of . In particular, we choose the prior for which the expected inference loss in the absensce of data is as large as possible, i.e. the prior with largest possible entropy .

Using information geometry, we show how to obtain such maximimum entropy distributions subject to any number of linear constraints of interest (which can be used to capture new knowledge).

17.2. Finite Alphabet Case

Abuse of notation: we will work with as the alphabet instead of the parameter . Bear with it.

with parameters chosen to satisfy the constraints.

Example 17.2 (Maximal entropy distribution given marginals). Consider a pair of jointly distributed random variables , each defined over the alphabet where the constraints are that the marginal distributions are prescribed.

Geometric approach: We have constraints, one for each marginal.

We can factorize into two exponentials and hence argue it’s independent. In particular, and .

Example 17.3 (Triple of jointly distributed random variables with pairwise marginals). Consider a triple of jointly distributed random variables defined as above with pairwise marginal distributions between and prescribed.

The maximum entropy distribution is a Markov chain!

Elaboration

17.3. Infinite Alphabet

The same argument generalizes our results for finite alphabets to discrete alphabets:

Theorem 17.1 (Maximum entropy distribution, uncountable alphabet). Among all distributions over a countable alphabet , the distribution given by the finite result Equation 17.3, when it exists, is the unique distribution in the linear family (Equation 17.2) with maximum entropy.

The uniform distribution need not be well-defined for continuous random variables (e..g. ). Also we haven’t developed information geometry for this case. Nevertheless, we have the following result that the exponential family form (that was derived for fthe finite alphabet case) maximizes entropy.

Theorem 17.2 (Maximum entropy distribution, uncountable alphabet). Consider all distributions over for which differential entropy is defined. Then in the linear family defined by the constraints (Equation 17.2), the distribution given by finite result Equation 17.3, when it exists21, is the unique distribution having maximum differential entropy.
Proof

Where since and belong to the same linear family they have the same expectations.

Uniqueness follows from Gibb’s inequality.

□

I.e. in all cases, we have a two-way correspondence.

  • Given a linear family, the max entropy distribution within it has an exponential family form.
  • Conversely, given an exponential family, its natural sufficient statistics define the linear constraints for which it is the max entropy solution

Examples:

  • Infintie alphabet does not have maximum entropy distribution.
  • with mean and variance is maximized by a normal distribution.
  • with fixed mean. Then it’s an exponential distribution
  • GIven continuous random variables with greater than zero, ten we get a multiavariate Gaussian with mean zero.
